cards.hook: You're a symphony of a trillion chemical reactions.
cards.caption: You're a symphony of a trillion chemical reactions, writing your life in symbols older than the Bronze Age. Consciousness never stops being strange. The kind of line my feed keeps.
cards.stance: "Just chemistry" isn't a downgrade of the mind — it's the most staggering thing about it.

cards.caption: "I think I think too much." Six words that name the overthinker's trap: the mind auditing itself into paralysis. Small line, deep cut. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Overthinking isn't deep thinking — it's thinking that never ships.

cards.hook: Monty Python turned philosophy into a football match.
cards.caption: Monty Python put Socrates, Kant and Nietzsche on a football pitch — and it teaches more epistemology than most 101 lectures. Comedy as the best gateway drug to philosophy. Found before the timeline buried it.
cards.stance: The funniest take on philosophy is also one of the most accurate.

cards.hook: "Reason is immortal, all else mortal." — Pythagoras
cards.caption: "Reason is immortal, all else mortal." Pythagoras bet logic outlives the body — the same wager behind every mind-independent math argument today. 2,500 years, still unsettled. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Do logical truths exist without minds? Pythagoras already picked a side.

cards.hook: Reading Kant with no internet = an ogre reading Ulysses.
cards.caption: Someone nailed pre-internet Kant: an ogre grinding through Ulysses, catching the surface, missing "remorse of conscience" entirely. We forget how hard dense texts were before explainers existed. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: The internet didn't make us dumber — it made the hardest books finally readable.

cards.hook: Is agnosticism a moral duty — or an intellectual cop-out?
cards.caption: Is agnosticism a rigorous moral duty or a cop-out? A philosopher and scientist go 21 minutes on it — Huxley's survival tactic, Russell's teapot, the ethics of permanent doubt. The kind of debate my feed keeps.
cards.stance: "I don't know" is either the most honest position or the laziest one — pick.

cards.hook: Voltaire wrote "illusion" — but did he mean "delusion"?
cards.caption: One reader argues Voltaire's "the first of all pleasures" makes more sense as delusion, not illusion — and that word choice flips the whole meaning. Translation is philosophy in miniature. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: One mistranslated word can quietly rewrite a thinker's entire point.
